column1: What is Active Directory (AD)?
column2: A directory service for Windows enterprise environments released in 2000, based on x.500 and LDAP protocols, providing centralized management of organizational resources including authentication, accounting, and authorization

column1: What does compromising Active Directory mean for an organization?
column2: Unrestricted access to all systems and data, violating confidentiality, integrity, and availability. It compromises all applications, systems, and data rather than just a single system

column1: What are the three main components of AD hierarchy?
column2: Domain (group of objects sharing same AD database), Tree (one or more domains grouped), Forest (group of multiple trees, topmost level)

column1: What is a Domain Controller's role?
column2: Provides authentication and authorization to services and users, has topmost priority and authority, administers the entire Active Directory

column1: What file is most critical in an AD environment and where is it stored?
column2: NTDS.DIT file, stored in %SystemRoot%\NTDS folder on domain controllers, contains all directory information

column1: What can a regular AD user account with no privileges enumerate?
column2: Domain computers, users, group information, default domain policy, functional levels, password policy, GPOs, Kerberos delegation, domain trusts, and ACLs

column1: What are the three authentication methods in Windows environments?
column2: Username/password (stored as password hashes), Kerberos tickets (Microsoft's implementation), and authentication over LDAP (via username/password or certificates)

column1: What is the KDC and what are its components?
column2: Key Distribution Center - a Kerberos service on DC that creates tickets. Components are Authentication Server (AS) and Ticket-Granting Server (TGS)

column1: What are the two types of Kerberos tickets?
column2: TGT (proof client submitted valid user info to KDC) and TGS (created for each service the client wants to access)

column1: What is the KRBTGT account?
column2: First account created in AD domain, disabled but vital for storing randomly generated keys as password hashes. Used to create KDC key proving TGT validity

column1: What groups have the most privileged access in AD?
column2: Domain Admins and Administrators (access all domain-joined machines), Enterprise Admins (permissions over all domains in forest)

column1: Why is the Account Operators group dangerous?
column2: Can reset user passwords without proper delegation, violating least privilege. Creates escalation path to Domain Admins through MSOL accounts

column1: What logon types leave credentials on accessed systems?
column2: All logon types except Network logon (type 3) leave username and password traces on authenticated systems

column1: What is SYSVOL?
column2: Network share on all Domain Controllers containing logon scripts, group policy data, and domain-wide data. All group policies stored in \\<DOMAIN>\SYSVOL\<DOMAIN>\Policies\

column1: What are the six important network ports in Windows environments?
column2: 53 (DNS), 88 (Kerberos), 135 (WMI/RPC), 137-139 & 445 (SMB), 389 & 636 (LDAP), 3389 (RDP), 5985 & 5986 (PowerShell Remoting/WinRM)

column1: What is an example of AD complexity issue?
column2: Nested group membership - difficult to track who is member of which groups, sometimes resulting in every Domain User indirectly being Domain Admin

column1: What is an example of AD design limitation?
column2: GPOs stored in SYSVOL accessed via SMB, which allows code execution. Valid credentials enable remote command execution on Domain Controllers over SMB

column1: What is an example of AD legacy issue?
column2: NetBIOS and LLMNR protocols enabled by default, broadcasting user credentials (usernames, passwords, hashes) on the wire when DNS fails

column1: What is Kerberoasting?
column2: Post-exploitation attack obtaining Kerberos TGS tickets encrypted with service account NTLM hash, performing offline password cracking to recover passwords

column1: What determines Kerberoasting success?
column2: Service account password strength and encryption algorithm used (AES slower to crack than RC4 or DES)

column1: What flag indicates RC4 encryption in Event ID 4769?
column2: Ticket Options field set for RC4 in the event log entry

column1: How to prevent Kerberoasting?
column2: Use 100+ random character passwords (127 max), implement Group Managed Service Accounts (GMSA) with automatic 127-char passwords, remove unnecessary SPNs

column1: What is a honeypot user for Kerberoasting?
column2: Relatively old user (2+ years) with strong password, unchanged for years, has privileges, has legitimate-looking SPN (IIS/SQL), no regular service tickets generated

column1: What is AS-REProasting?
column2: Attack obtaining crackable hashes for user accounts with "Do not require Kerberos preauthentication" property enabled

column1: How to prevent AS-REProasting?
column2: Only use "Do not require preauthentication" when needed, assign separate password policy requiring 20+ characters for affected users

column1: What Event ID indicates Kerberos authentication ticket generation?
column2: Event ID 4768, generated for every Kerberos authentication but useful when correlating with known good logins or unusual VLANs

column1: What is GPP Passwords vulnerability?
column2: Group Policy Preferences stored credentials in XML files in SYSVOL using published AES key, allowing anyone to decrypt them

column1: When was GPP Passwords patched and what does patch do?
column2: KB2962486 in 2014, prevents caching new credentials but doesn't clear existing ones from pre-2014 environments

column1: What Event ID indicates file access for GPP detection?
column2: Event ID 4663 when user reads XML file containing credentials

column1: What is a GPO?
column2: Group Policy Object - virtual collection of policy settings with unique name, linked to OUs to apply settings to objects within

column1: What makes a GPO vulnerable?
column2: Permissions allowing "Authenticated Users" or "Domain Users" to modify it, or misconfigured NTFS permissions on deployed files

column1: What Event ID indicates GPO modification?
column2: Event ID 5136 when Directory Service Changes auditing enabled

column1: What are common locations for credentials in network shares?
column2: Scripts and configuration files (batch, cmd, PowerShell, conf, ini, config) on shares accessible to many users

column1: What are four reasons shares become accessible to everyone?
column2: Admin opens share to everyone, admin unaware folder is shared, temporary share forgotten, misconception about hidden shares (ending in $)

column1: What makes hidden shares (ending in $) insecure?
column2: Explorer doesn't display them but other tools do. Not a security measure, just hidden from default view

column1: What Event IDs indicate failed authentication attempts?
column2: Event IDs 4625 (failed logon), 4771 (Kerberos pre-auth failure), 4776 (credential validation failure)

column1: What properties commonly contain exposed passwords?
column2: Description and Info properties of user objects, readable by all domain users despite admin assumptions

column1: How to prevent credentials in object properties?
column2: Continuous assessment, employee education, automate user creation to reduce manual handling

column1: What is DCSync attack?
column2: Impersonating Domain Controller to perform replication with targeted DC, extracting password hashes from Active Directory

column1: What permissions enable DCSync?
column2: Replicating Directory Changes and "Replicating Directory Changes All" assigned to user or computer account

column1: How to prevent DCSync?
column2: Use RPC Firewall to block/allow specific RPC calls, only allowing replications from Domain Controllers

column1: What Event ID detects DCSync?
column2: Event ID 4662 generated for each DC replication. Alert when initiator is not a Domain Controller

column1: What properties in Event 4662 indicate DCSync?
column2: Property 1131f6aa-9c07-11d1-f79f-00c04fc2dcd2 or 1131f6ad-9c07-11d1-f79f-00c04fc2dcd2 present in event

column1: What is Golden Ticket attack?
column2: Creating forged Kerberos TGTs using KRBTGT password hash, acting as Domain Controller to create tickets for any user with any privileges

column1: What makes Golden Ticket powerful?
column2: KRBTGT hash signs all Kerberos tickets, so forged tickets are trusted domain-wide. Allows escalation from child to parent domain

column1: How to prevent Golden Ticket?
column2: Block privileged users from authenticating to any device, periodically reset KRBTGT password, enforce SIDHistory filtering between domains

column1: Why reset KRBTGT password twice?
column2: Password history stores 2 most recent passwords. Resetting twice clears old passwords, preventing DC replication with old password

column1: What is recommended time between KRBTGT password resets?
column2: At least 10 hours apart (maximum user ticket lifetime) to avoid breaking services

column1: What Event ID shows successful logon for Golden Ticket detection?
column2: Event IDs 4624 and 4625 (successful/failed logon), correlate with user behavior, location, and Privileged Access Workstations

column1: What is Kerberos Delegation?
column2: Allows application to access resources on different server on behalf of user without giving service account direct access

column1: What are three types of Kerberos Delegation?
column2: Unconstrained Delegation (most permissive), Constrained Delegation (specific services), Resource-based Delegation (configured on target computer)

column1: What makes Constrained Delegation vulnerable?
column2: Can request Kerberos ticket for any user and perform protocol transition to services not explicitly configured

column1: How to prevent Kerberos Delegation abuse?
column2: Configure "Account is sensitive and cannot be delegated" for privileged users, add to Protected Users group, use strong passwords

column1: What is the PrinterBug?
column2: RPC functions RpcRemoteFindFirstPrinterChangeNotification(Ex) can force remote machine to authenticate to any reachable machine with TGT

column1: What is Microsoft's stance on PrinterBug?
column2: Will not fix as it is "by-design" behavior

column1: What are four ways to exploit PrinterBug?
column2: Relay to another DC for DCSync, force connection to Unconstrained Delegation server, relay to ADCS for certificate, relay for Resource-Based Delegation

column1: How to prevent PrinterBug?
column2: Disable Print Spooler on non-printing servers, set RegisterSpoolerRemoteRpcEndPoint registry key to 2 to block remote requests

column1: What registry key prevents PrinterBug while keeping service running?
column2: RegisterSpoolerRemoteRpcEndPoint set to 2 (1 enables, 2 disables remote RPC endpoint)

column1: What are coercing attacks?
column2: Attacks using various RPC functions to force remote machines to authenticate to attacker-controlled machine

column1: What tool exploits all known vulnerable RPC functions?
column2: Coercer tool, developed to exploit multiple vulnerable RPC functions simultaneously

column1: How to prevent coercing attacks?
column2: Implement RPC firewall to block dangerous RPC functions, or block outbound ports 139/445 from DCs except required machines

column1: What is Unconstrained Delegation?
column2: Most permissive delegation allowing account to delegate to any service, caches TGTs in memory of trusted server

column1: How does coercing with Unconstrained Delegation work?
column2: Force DC to connect to UD server, TGT cached in UD server memory, export with Rubeus/Mimikatz, use for authentication

column1: What are ACLs in Active Directory?
column2: Access Control Lists - tables defining trustees with access to specific objects and their access type

column1: What is an ACE?
column2: Access Control Entry - defines trustee and access type within an ACL. Multiple ACEs can exist per object

column1: What is a common ACL misconfiguration?
column2: All Domain Users added as Administrators to servers, Everyone can modify all objects, all users access LAPS passwords

column1: What can full rights over user object enable?
column2: Add SPN for Kerberoasting, reset password to inherit all user's rights and privileges

column1: What can full control over computer object enable?
column2: Obtain LAPS password for local Administrator, abuse Resource-Based Kerberos Delegation to authenticate as anyone

column1: How to prevent ACL abuse?
column2: Continuous assessment, educate privileged employees, automate access management, assign privileges only to admin accounts

column1: What Event ID indicates user account modification?
column2: Event ID 4738 "A user account was changed" but doesn't show specific changed properties

column1: What Event ID indicates password reset?
column2: Event ID 4724 generated when ACL abuse leads to password reset

column1: What Event ID indicates computer account modification?
column2: Event ID 4742, indicates account used maliciously but limited information on what changed

column1: What is AD CS?
column2: Active Directory Certificate Services - PKI infrastructure for issuing and managing certificates in Windows environments

column1: What are advantages of certificate-based authentication?
column2: Valid 1+ years, password reset doesn't invalidate certificate, can forge certificates for any user, compromising CA enables golden certificates

column1: What is ESC1 vulnerability?
column2: Domain escalation via no issuance requirements, enrollable client authentication OID templates, CT_FLAG_ENROLLEE_SUPPLIES_SUBJECT flag

column1: What makes certificate template vulnerable to ESC1?
column2: All domain users can enroll, CT_FLAG_ENROLLEE_SUPPLIES_SUBJECT allows requester to specify SAN, no manager approval, usable for client authentication

column1: How to prevent ESC1?
column2: Disable CT_FLAG_ENROLLEE_SUPPLIES_SUBJECT flag, require CA manager approval before issuing certificates, regularly scan with Certify

column1: What Event IDs indicate certificate request and issuance?
column2: Event ID 4886 (received request) and 4887 (issued certificate) logged on CA machine

column1: Where can you view issued certificates and SAN?
column2: CA's list of issued certificates, need to open individual certificate to view Subject Alternative Name

column1: What is ESC8 vulnerability?
column2: Relaying authentication to ADCS HTTP endpoint to obtain certificate, known as NTLM relay to certificate services

column1: How does ESC8 attack work?
column2: Use PrinterBug/Coercer to force machine connection, relay to ADCS HTTP endpoint, obtain certificate for coerced machine

column1: How to prevent ESC8?
column2: Prevent successful coercion, enforce HTTPS on ADCS web enrollment (prevents relay), regularly scan for PKI issues

column1: What indicates relayed certificate request in Event 4886?
column2: Template name mentioned as part of request (not present when DC requests its own certificate)

column1: What must be done if AD forest is compromised?
column2: Reset all user passwords, revoke all certificates, reset KRBTGT password twice in every domain (10+ hours apart)
